speaker: doctor
text: Good morning. Your fasting blood glucose today is 4.9 mmol/L — that’s within the normal range.

speaker: doctor
text: Your hemoglobin level is 178 g/L, which is on the higher end of normal for an adult male.

speaker: patient
text: Thanks, Doctor. I’ve been feeling a bit tired lately, and my ankles have been swelling more than usual.

speaker: patient
text: Yes — I’ve noticed lower limb edema over the past few days, especially by evening.

speaker: doctor
text: Given your history and current findings, I’m diagnosing you with Coronary Artery Disease — we’ll need to optimize your cardiac management.

speaker: doctor
text: Your body temperature is 37.2 °C — completely normal, no sign of infection.

speaker: patient
text: I’ve also had some shortness of breath when climbing stairs. And my legs feel heavy all day.

speaker: doctor
text: Let me check your vitals again. I’ll listen to your heart and lungs, and assess the edema further.

speaker: doctor
text: Your heart rate is currently 95 bpm — slightly elevated, which may reflect volume overload or early decompensation.

speaker: patient
text: I’ve been taking my metoprolol as prescribed, but I missed two doses last week because I felt dizzy.

speaker: doctor
text: Just rechecked — your heart rate is now 68 bpm, likely reflecting the beta-blocker effect after rest. That’s reassuring.

speaker: doctor
text: The lower limb edema suggests possible fluid retention. We should evaluate your renal function and review your diuretic regimen.

speaker: patient
text: I’ve been using furosemide 20 mg daily, but sometimes skip it if I’m not swollen in the morning.

speaker: doctor
text: Skipping doses can lead to rebound fluid retention. Let’s adjust your plan — I’m going to switch you to bumetanide 1 mg twice daily, starting tomorrow.

speaker: doctor
text: Your latest hemoglobin is 161 g/L — down from 178 g/L earlier this morning. That drop may reflect hemodilution due to fluid retention.

speaker: patient
text: Oh — so the swelling could be affecting my blood counts? That makes sense.

speaker: doctor
text: Exactly. We’ll repeat labs in one week, including BNP and electrolytes, and schedule an echocardiogram next month.

speaker: doctor
text: Your random blood glucose just came back at 7.6 mmol/L — higher than your fasting value this morning. Let’s discuss your carbohydrate intake and consider adjusting your metformin dose.

speaker: patient
text: Wait — that doesn’t match the hemoglobin reading you mentioned earlier. You said it was 178 g/L before — that number hasn’t changed for me.

speaker: patient
text: And my fasting glucose was definitely 4.9 mmol/L this morning — I checked it myself right before coming in, same as your first reading.
